user_query:
I'm a high school student doing remote learning, and I need extra guidance for a simple science data analysis assignment. Please download the classic Iris data set from the official UCI Machine Learning Repository (the original "Iris" data set by R. A. Fisher; the data file is named "iris.data" on the UCI site under the ICS domain). Do not use a mirror or an alternative source. Save the raw file as data/iris.data, then create a cleaned CSV at data/iris.csv with a header row and these columns in this exact order: sepal_length, sepal_width, petal_length, petal_width, species. Parse the four measurements as floating-point numbers (centimeters) and species as a string (values like Iris-setosa, Iris-versicolor, Iris-virginica). After preparing the data, produce the following outputs under the analysis/ folder:

1) Species summary statistics (compute basic statistics and aggregates): analysis/species_summary.csv with one row per species and columns: species, count, mean_sepal_length, std_sepal_length, mean_sepal_width, std_sepal_width, mean_petal_length, std_petal_length, mean_petal_width, std_petal_width.

2) Correlation matrix (compute basic statistics): analysis/correlation.csv for the four numeric measurements (sepal_length, sepal_width, petal_length, petal_width). Include a header row and four rows so each cell contains the Pearson correlation between the row and column variables.

3) Filter, sort, and rank (make these steps explicit and essential): Filter the cleaned data to only rows where petal_length >= 4.5 and sepal_width >= 2.5. Sort the filtered rows by petal_length descending, then sepal_length descending, then petal_width descending. Save the top 30 rows (or all if fewer than 30 match) to analysis/filtered_top.csv with the same five columns as data/iris.csv.

4) Ranking by a composite score: Add a new column size_score = petal_length + sepal_length to the full cleaned data. Create analysis/top10_overall.csv containing the top 10 rows with the highest size_score, sorted by size_score descending, tie-breaking by petal_width descending, then sepal_width descending. Include columns: species, sepal_length, sepal_width, petal_length, petal_width, size_score. Also create analysis/top5_by_species.csv listing the top 5 rows within each species by the same ranking rules (group by species, rank within each group, and concatenate the three groups in any order), with the same columns as top10_overall.csv plus a column rank_within_species (1 is best within its species).

5) A short plain-text summary: analysis/report.txt that states: (a) the total number of rows loaded from data/iris.csv, (b) the count per species (these must match the counts in analysis/species_summary.csv), (c) the species with the highest mean petal_length from analysis/species_summary.csv, and (d) the species and size_score of the single highest-scoring row in analysis/top10_overall.csv. Include one line identifying the source as "UCI Machine Learning Repository — Iris data set".

Please make the Internet download an explicit step (no bundling the data). Provide a reproducible script at scripts/iris_analysis.py that, when run from the workspace root, downloads the data and generates all outputs exactly at the paths specified above. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
